narration.default: Your workspace already has the html, head, and body blocks — every webpage starts that way (you built this in the Hello Web tutorial). Today we just focus on what we add INSIDE the body.
Drag a heading inside the body. Keep the level at 1 and type My Favourite Animal.

Some tags carry extra information on the OPEN tag itself — like an tag carrying the picture's web address and a description. Those extras are called ATTRIBUTES — a name and a value, joined by an equals sign. The block has a slot for each one.

When you grab the img block in a moment, notice something different — it's a STACK shape, not a C-shape. Most blocks (like p, h1, div) have a SLOT in the middle for content to nest inside. img doesn't. That's because the img tag is SELF-CLOSING — it stands alone, holding all its information in attributes (src and alt). Other self-closing tags work the same way: input (the form box) and br (a line break). They don't WRAP content — they ARE the content. When you see a stack-shape block in the toolbox, that's your hint that the tag is self-closing.
Drag the image block below the heading inside the body.
Click the source field on the image block, called src. Replace the text with h t t p s colon slash slash picsum dot photos slash i d slash two three seven slash four hundred slash three hundred.
Click the alt field. Type A puppy looking at the camera. The alt describes the picture in words. It helps people who can't see the image understand it.

Alt text shows when the image can't load — broken connection, wrong filename, anything. It also helps screen readers describe pictures to people who can't see them. Even when everything works perfectly, the alt is doing its job invisibly.
Click the View tab. Do you see the puppy below your heading?
An image has two parts: the source, called src, which tells where the picture comes from, and the alt, which describes it.
